instruction: indexed and sorted BAM files as Input files for "coverage" command (Tool: CheckM)
input: Dear Donovan, Hello, this is Min-Soo Kim who is interested in your CheckM tool. I am trying to produce coverage profile from my data using "coverage" command. However, I keep getting errors with .bam files, .bai files or .sorted files as follows: "[Error] BAM file is either unsorted or not indexed: DIRECTORY/FILE_NAME.bam" "[Error] BAM file is either unsorted or not indexed: DIRECTORY/FILE_NAME.sorted.bai" "[Error] BAM file is either unsorted or not indexed: DIRECTORY/FILE_NAME.sorted" Here is the processes what I did to get sorted and indexed BAM files, 1. I used bowtie2 with paired-end read files to generate read-mapping SAM files, and converted to BAM files using samtools-v1.3.1 > samtools view -b INPUT.sam OUTPUT.bam 2. OUTPUT.bam files were sorted and indexed. > samtools sort INPUT.bam -o OUTPUT.sorted --output_fomt BAM > samtools index OUTPUT.sorted 3. I got .bai files, and put in OUTPUT.sorted.bai files with "coverage" command. > checkm coverage -x fa -a 90 -t 20 BIN_FOLDER/ OUTPUT.file FILE1.sorted.bai FILE2.sorted.bai FILE3.sorted.bai ... Could you help me figure out what is wrong with me? Thank you for your help. Min-Soo Kim
output: Thank you for your response. As you assumed, I resolved the issue. Using samtools-v1.3.1, I did not get "sorted.BAM file" from "BAM file". I changed to samtools-v1.2, and could get "sorted and indexed BAM file". I found that 'coverage' command works with "sorted BAM file" as an input file when all three files ("BAM file + "sorted BAM file" + "sorted.bai file") are put in the same folder. Every time I use the CheckM tool, I appreciate you who developed this valuable and practical tool. Min-Soo Kim On Thu, Aug 4, 2016 at 1:35 AM, Donovan Parks notifications@github.com wrote: > Hello. I assume you closed this issue as you resolved the issue. Just in > case others look this up, the problem is that the coverage command > expects the *.bam file, and not the *.bam.bai files as input. > > — > You are receiving this because you modified the open/close state. > Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub > https://github.com/Ecogenomics/CheckM/issues/71#issuecomment-237273992, > or mute the thread > https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AT2AiqmgtyJL0qkb0crKmaYyYXxLKXaAks5qcMNYgaJpZM4JbTsP > .

instruction: pplacer will crash on machine with insufficient memory (Tool: CheckM)
input: pplacer will crash on machines with <40GB. This case should be identified within CheckM by checking if the file ./storage/tree/concatenated.pplacer.json was successfully created. If not, CheckM should report that there is likely a memory issue and gracefully terminate.
output: Regarding to the memory issue, I just talked with another colleague in our department, and he used to ask for even more memory "#PBS -l nodes=1:ppn=16:HIGHMEM #PBS -l mem=500gb" but still got the same error...my best diagnosis is something is funky about pplacer; could be that our version (pplacer/1.1.alpha17-linux) is out-dated or something? instruction: Whether there have pre-train code and re-annotating scripts for CheckM2 model on custom database? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: So greate work for checkm2. This is issue based on #114, I intend to use CheckM2 based on my custom database but in vain. And I saw the issue #114, it shoud re-annotate and re-train the CheckM2 models. However, it seems that it has not provided in the offical GitHub rep. Whether there have pre-train code for training CheckM2 models for custom database. And the re-annotating scripts whether checkm2 team can kindly provide them. I can not find them in the official GitHub rep.
output: You can adapt the code and scripts from the checkm2 updater repo: https://github.com/chklovski/checkm2_supplementary Keep in mind that 1) this will take a substantial amount of effort if you are regenerating the entire training set (you need to re-generate hundreds of thousands of simulated genomes, and annotate them with KEGG Ids using DIAMOND, which is a substantial amount of compute), and 2) this will have no relation to the official CheckM2 release or publication. Can I ask what the purpose is? Generally there is no point using anything other than complete genomes for synthetic genome generation (because you need to know exactly their completeness/contamination, which you can only do if you're starting with a 100% complete, 0% contaminated genome), and CheckM2 already includes most existing complete RefSeq genomes (currently up to GTDB release 207, upcoming version up to release 226)?

instruction: Is there way to do lineage_wf as checkm v1 (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Dear CheckM development team, I moved from checkm v1 to checkm2. The performance was pretty good. It seemed to me that however "lineage_wf" function is not present in checkm2. I wondered if that's true and if so is there a way to do it in checkm2? Thank you! best, sishuo
output: No, CheckM2 uses a completely different approach to CheckM1 which does not rely on marker genes - see the CheckM2 publication or more details.

instruction: Reliable completeness scores for endosymbiont genomes? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hi there, I was wondering if the developers had any insight into whether the CheckM2 predictions might be less reliable for abnormally small bacterial genomes, such as obligate endosymbionts with streamlined genomes. For instance, I see based on the summary in GTDB that many genomes in the genus _Sulcia_ failed quality checks: https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org/searches?s=al&q=Sulcia, and similarly for _Wolbachia_: https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org/searches?s=al&q=Wolbachia. Similarly, all _Hodgkinia_ genomes failed quality checks: https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org/searches?s=al&q=Hodgkinia. By eye it looks like at least many of these failures are due to low completeness. Do you think the training data (presumably mainly comprised of free-living bacteria) could cause a downward bias when predicting completeness for bacteria with streamlined/degenerating genomes? For more context, you can see my post about this observation on the AllTheBacteria GitHub: https://github.com/AllTheBacteria/AllTheBacteria/issues/54 Thanks, Gavin
output: Hi, CheckM2 has actually been trained with endosymbionts in its training set and should outperform CheckM1 substantially when it comes to predicting completeness and contamination. This appears to be the case for at least some of the symbionts in GTDB you referenced - see e.g. https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org/genome?gid=GCA_000021505.1 or https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org/genome?gid=GCA_000831405.1 the 'failed quality check' used by GTDB I believe includes metrics other than CheckM2 Completeness/Contamination, which is why those two particular genomes fail even though CheckM2 metrics for them are excellent. Obviously CheckM2 is not perfect for all endosymbionts, but hopefully it will improve scores as we update the models with more high-quality endosymbiont genomes.

instruction: Is the reference diamond database periodically being updated? How can I check if my database is still up to date? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Aparrently the uniref100.KO.1.dmnd database has already beed updated at least once in the past. Are further future updates likely? If so, what is the best way to check if a new database version is available and/or if the currently installed one is outdated?
output: Hi, the updates of the reference database are purely due to the Diamond version, the content remains the same. The diamond versions are tied to specific CheckM2 versions due to combinations of different pre-requisites, but CheckM2 should determine automatically which version to pull. There is no difference in the content - it's all the same uniref100 database annotated with KEGG KO's. You can see the specifics in the https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/blob/main/checkm2/version/version_hashes_{version}.json file

input: Hi, I did a test run as recommended, however the results are quite different from the readme. ``` Name Completeness Contamination Completeness_Model_Used Translation_Table_Used TEST1 100.00 0.74 Neural Network (Specific Model) 11 TEST2 98.54 0.21 Neural Network (Specific Model) 11 TEST3 98.75 0.51 Neural Network (Specific Model) 11 ``` Is that normal? Also, should there be a taxonomy in that output? running 1.0.2 with diamond database uniref100.KO.1.dmnd, not sure if that helps.
output: Apologies for the confusion, the numbers from the README are for CheckM1 (for comparison). I will fix this as soon as I finish the next version release (imminent). The genomes used in testrun are three complete, circular genomes and it is expected that CheckM2 will give high quality scores for them. Your numbers are correct.

instruction: Can Checkm2 be fine-tuned for euMAGs? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hello, Just curious about whether CheckM2 can be fine-tuned to assess the eukaryotic microbial genome quality. Andy
output: Hi, In principle you can use the same approach, in practice you would at least need to account for: 1) different gene calling for Eukaryotes, which is substantially more complex, and 2) very limited nature of complete, intact sequenced eukaryotic genomes for machine learning training purposes. At the moment just using BUSCO is likely to be a lot easier and more straightforward as this tool explicitly works on eukaryotic quality assessment.

instruction: Error in DIAMOND execution (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hello, I bumbed into problem with Diamond ``` INFO: Running CheckM2 version 1.0.1 INFO: Custom database path provided for predict run. Checking database at uniref100.KO.1.dmnd... INFO: Running quality prediction workflow with 8 threads. INFO: Calling genes in 1 bins with 8 threads: Finished processing 1 of 1 (100.00%) bins. INFO: Calculating metadata for 1 bins with 8 threads: Finished processing 1 of 1 (100.00%) bin metadata. INFO: Annotating input genomes with DIAMOND using 8 threads INFO: Processing DIAMOND output ERROR: No DIAMOND annotation was generated. Exiting ``` execution command: `singularity run quay.io-biocontainers-checkm2-1.0.1--pyh7cba7a3_0.img checkm2 predict --threads 8 --input bins -x fa --output-directory binner13_checkm_output --database_path uniref100.KO.1.dmnd` bins folder contains 1 bin.fa (attached) [bins.fa.gz](https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/files/14192776/bins.fa.gz) Should checkm2 generate empty output in that case? Could you explain what is wrong with DIAMOND execution? Thanks! Best, Kate
output: Hi, The problem here lies with translation when using prodigal - the ~940Kb bin generates only 31 predicted proteins using prodigal, most of which are tiny. As a result, DIAMOND cannot confidently assign any KEGG ID's to any of the protein fragments predicted, generates no output, which leads to the CheckM2 error as that's the only bin in the input. Is it possible the bin contains non-prokaryotic DNA that doesn't play well with prodigal?

instruction: How to remove the contamination sequence from MAGs (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hello, both checkM1 and checkM2 are excellent softwares to estimate the quality of MAGs. However, is there any way to remove the contamination sequences in the MAG? It seems that checkM1/checkM2 could only tell us the quality of a MAG.
output: I don't think it should be CheckM2's job to remove contamination, as that is likely to just bias fitting MAG composition to CheckM2 scores instead of using proper binning metric (coverage, tetranucleotide identity, etc). If you're after tools to improve MAGs, you could try something like GUNC or MAGpurify.

instruction: Can I use protein fasta as input? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hello, I want to know whether I can use protein fasta files as input? Protein fasta files were downloaded from NCBI with ".faa" extension. They are annotated by other tools based on genome sequences. Or, maybe I can place them in the output file (protein_files) and only reuse protein_files and generate new diamond output? So far, I have to have "protein_files" and "diamond_output" when I use "--reuse". If I only provide "protein_files", checkM2 will report error. Looking forward to your response. Thank you! Ruolin He
output: Hi, yes you can. Specify the extension with `-x faa` and indicate that you're passing predicted proteins by using `--genes`.

instruction: Small test diamond db (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hi, Thank you for checkm2; it's very helpful. We are currently working on an nf-core module for this tool and I was wondering if it's possible to create a smaller Diamond database for a unit test. Could you please provide some guidance? Cheers
output: Hi, What's the purpose of using a smaller db for the testcase? Currently CheckM2 forces a checksum on the database it uses and throws an error if it's not the expected database for a given CheckM2 version. That's because the training & testing is performed using the same database, and altering the database can alter the expected distribution of protein annotations for the genomes CheckM2 has been trained on, leading to inaccurate predictions. An example of this: If you were to simply filter the existing database (uniref100 annotated with kegg ID's) down to uniref50, and run existing CheckM2 models (bypassing checksum check), predictions of completeness & contamination change. In edge cases, for example for highly novel genomes coming from highly reduced lineages, they can lose as much as 10-15% completeness, because some of the annotations CheckM2 has been trained to include in its calculations are simply not there in the database. Retraining the CheckM2 machine learning models on synthetic genomes annotated using the new database would likely get around this by changing expected distribution of annotations. However, you would need to re-generate annotation for all ~1+ million synthetic genomes CheckM2 uses in order to retrain the models, and it wouldn't be reproducible for other users on stock CheckM2 database & models. We can do this centrally for a major release if there's a good benefit of e.g. a newer database with better annotations, but I'm not sure that would be useful for your testcase.

instruction: Include more stats (Tool: CheckM2)
input: It would be awesome if you could include more stats in the output file, particularly strain heterogeneity (as in Checkm) and contig counts.
output: Hi, strain heterogeneity is not something CheckM2 calculates, as it does not use counts of single-copy marker genes occurring more than once, which is the basis for the strain heterogeneity calculation in CheckM1. Contig count stats are implemented into CheckM2 as of version 1.0.2.

instruction: Big Data Analysis (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hi, I have a question regarding the data capacity limitations of CheckM2. I'm interested in knowing the maximum data size or number of genomes that can be processed in a single run. Could you please provide some information on this? Thank you!
output: Hi, CheckM2 is designed to do bulk processing of many genomes at once. I've run it on 225,000 at once to assess the entirety of GTDB release 202, which finished successfully, so you should be able to run it on tens to hundreds of thousands of genomes at a time without a big spike in computing requirements.

instruction: specify dependecy versions (Tool: CheckM2)
input: The current [conda env yaml](https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/blob/main/checkm2.yml) doesn't specify the specific allowable versions for some dependencies, but this can lead to conflicts, such as issues with specific versions of numpy, grpcio, and scipy. See https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/issues/38 for the numpy-grpcio conflict, and see https://github.com/scipy/scipy/releases/tag/v1.10.0 for the numpy requirements for the most recent version of scipy.
output: I tried explicitly running the setup as in the README: ``` git clone --recursive https://github.com/chklovski/checkm2.git --branch 1.0.1 cd checkm2 mamba env create -f checkm2.yml python setup.py install ``` This results in the following error: ``` error: grpcio 1.32.0 is installed but grpcio>=1.48.2 is required by {'tensorboard'} ``` However, `pip install "grpcio>=1.48.2"` results in the following: ``` Requirement already satisfied: grpcio>=1.48.2 in /home/gitpod/.conda/envs/checkm2/lib/python3.8/site-packages (1.52.1) ``` ...and `python3.8/site-packages/` contains both `grpcio-1.52.1.dist-info` and `grpcio-1.32.0-py3.8-linux-x86_64.egg`. `mamba env create -f checkm2.yml` is installing `grpcio 1.52.1`, so `python setup.py install` must be additionally installing `grpcio-1.32.0`. I'm using `Ubuntu 20.04.5` for this test.

instruction: [Dependency Request] Generalize the dependency versions (Tool: CheckM2)
input: I'm trying to establish this in my https://github.com/jolespin/veba pipeline and the prokaryotic binning module has quite a few dependencies I need to work around. Here's the current dependency restrictions: ``` - python >=3.6, <3.9 - scikit-learn =0.23.2 - h5py =2.10.0 - numpy =1.19.2 - diamond =2.0.4 - tensorflow >=2.1.0, <2.6.0 - lightgbm =3.2.1 - pandas <=1.4.0 - scipy - prodigal >=2.6.3 - setuptools - requests - packaging - tqdm ``` #### Diamond In particular, can Diamond allow for more versions? Any version compatible with db version 3? ``` (checkm2_env) [jespinoz@exp-15-05 jcl110]$ diamond dbinfo -d db/checkm2/CheckM2_database/uniref100.KO.1.dmnd diamond v2.0.4.142 (C) Max Planck Society for the Advancement of Science Documentation, support and updates available at http://www.diamondsearch.org Database format version = 3 Diamond build = 142 Sequences = 6518230 Letters = 2584051404 ``` According to this thread: https://github.com/bbuchfink/diamond/issues/313#issuecomment-554949580 Any of the upcoming versions should be able to handle this database version. ##### Sklearn Also, `sklearn` is only used for `MinMaxScaler` (https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/blob/d40aef910f24bf7e479e5f7ff1ed326f30d2884b/checkm2/modelProcessing.py) I noticed that it's not actually used here: https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/blob/d40aef910f24bf7e479e5f7ff1ed326f30d2884b/checkm2/modelProcessing.py#L19 Though, it looks like one of the models might have it in the backend: ``` /expanse/projects/jcl110/anaconda3/envs/VEBA-binning-prokaryotic2_env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/sklearn/base.py:329: UserWarning: Trying to unpickle estimator MinMaxScaler from version 0.23.2 when using version 1.0.2. This might lead to breaking code or invalid results. Use at your own risk. For more info please refer to: ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "/expanse/projects/jcl110/anaconda3/envs/VEBA-binning-prokaryotic2_env/bin/checkm2", line 265, in <module> predictor.prediction_wf(False, 'auto',
output: Hi, sorry for the later reply. MinMaxScaler is used for feature vector transformation: `` def run_prediction_specific(self, vector_array, specific_model_vector_len):`` `` scaled_vector = self.minmax_scaler.transform(vector_array)`` Currently this requires a specific sklearn version, although I'm looking into alternatives. In general I've constrained the versions of several dependencies because of annoying conflicts you run into with more recent versions of numpy/scipy and especially tensorflow. Is it possible for you to have a separate (conda) environment where checkm2 dependencies are isolated from anything else? That would probably make things easier.

instruction: grpcio dependency: No such file or directory: 'cc' (Tool: CheckM2)
input: ## Steps to reproduce: env.yaml file: ``` channels: - conda-forge - bioconda - defaults dependencies: - python=3.9 - scikit-learn=0.23.2 - h5py=2.10.0 - numpy=1.19.2 - diamond=2.0.4 - tensorflow>=2.1.0,<2.6.0 - lightgbm=3.2.1 - pandas<=1.4.0 - scipy - prodigal>=2.6.3 - setuptools - requests - packaging - tqdm ``` Dockerfile: ``` FROM mambaorg/micromamba:1.1.0 ARG MAMBA_DOCKERFILE_ACTIVATE=1 COPY --chown=$MAMBA_USER:$MAMBA_USER env.yaml /tmp/env.yaml RUN micromamba install -y -n base -f /tmp/env.yaml git && \ micromamba clean --all --yes RUN git clone --recursive https://github.com/chklovski/checkm2.git && \ cd checkm2 && \ python setup.py install && \ cd ../ && rm -rf checkm2 ``` Build: ``` docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t checkm2:1.0.0 . ``` ## Error ```bash Installed /opt/conda/lib/python3.9/site-packages/wheel-0.38.4-py3.9.egg Searching for grpcio~=1.32.0 Reading https://pypi.org/simple/grpcio/ Downloading https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/0e/5f/eeb402746a65839acdec78b7e757635f5e446138cc1d68589dfa32cba593/grpcio-1.32.0.tar.gz#sha256=01d3046fe980be25796d368f8fc5ff34b7cf5e1444f3789a017a7fe794465639 Best match: grpcio 1.32.0 Processing grpcio-1.32.0.tar.gz Writing /tmp/easy_install-eop8d0xx/grpcio-1.32.0/setup.cfg Running grpcio-1.32.0/setup.py -q bdist_egg --dist-dir /tmp/easy_install-eop8d0xx/grpcio-1.32.0/egg-dist-tmp-s4mfdn9f /tmp/easy_install-eop8d0xx/grpcio-1.32.0/src/python/grpcio/commands.py:104: SyntaxWarning: "is not" with a literal. Did you mean "!="? if exit_code is not 0: error: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cc' ```
output: If you'd like to set up CI for the repo, this action should get you most of the way there: ``` name: CheckM2 on: [push, pull_request] jobs: build: name: build (${{ matrix.python-version }}, ${{ matrix.os }}) runs-on: ubuntu-latest env: DATADIR: data strategy: matrix: python-version: [3.9] build_type: [Release] compiler: [g++-9] include: - compiler: g++-9 steps: - name: Checkout repository uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Checkout submodules run: git submodule update --init --recursive - uses: conda-incubator/setup-miniconda@v2 with: miniconda-version: 'latest' auto-update-conda: true mamba-version: "*" python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }} channels: conda-forge,bioconda environment-file: checkm2.yml activate-environment: checkm2 - name: package install shell: bash -l {0} working-directory: ${{runner.workspace}}/checkm2/ run: | python setup.py install - name: checkm2 unit tests shell: bash -l {0} working-directory: ${{runner.workspace}}/checkm2/tests run: | pytest [checkm unit tests] - name: checkm2 CLI tests shell: bash -l {0} working-directory: ${{runner.workspace}}/checkm2/tests run: | checkm2 -h checkm2 predict -h ```

instruction: Increase protein search stringency? (Low-homology problem) (Tool: CheckM2)
input: The original checkm used hmmsearch with an HMM database of marker genes. This is arguably the most accurate and sensitive method for detecting remote homology in protein alignment, particularly when a sequence is notably diverged from any individual reference sequence in the database but still maintains the characteristic conservation pattern in the highly conserved sites (identified in an MSA). The second most sensitive protein search is blastp and its ilk (pairwise sensitive alignment). In third place is the default "fast" search mode of diamond. The difference between diamond's default "fast" and its --ultra-sensitive is well-documented (including in its own publication), with difference increasing with sequence divergence. The difference between pairwise blastp and a well-constructed HMM from a rigorous MSA is also well understood in phylogenetics and remote homology detection. Can you justify why hmms have been dropped in favor of pairwise protein alignments, and further can you explain why the default "fast" mode is used in the more-heuristic diamond aligner rather than blastp or the near-equivalent (but faster) --ultra-sensitive mode of diamond? Further, I see uniref100 was used (which does not contain many proteins found in metagenomes, e.g. MGNify) as a proxy for annotating proteins with KEGG IDs (which also relies on assignment of those original uniref proteins from likely an even older version of KEGG using further heuristic assignments). Propagating heuristics from the initial KEGG assignments to those proteins, and then from those proteins to new proteins, has potential for error especially with more distant homologies (which are less accurate anyway with the diamond default blastp fast parameters). Most strikingly, these multiple layers of abstractions are unnecessary when the actual KEGG HMMs already exist publicly (see KoFamKoala's FTP) and are the standard in assigning KEGG IDs de novo. It seems checkm2 is going through a long game of telephone, cha
output: Hi, thank you for your interest in CheckM2. This was a largely practical and empirical decision. During initial validation, we compared using DIAMOND-based KEGG annotations with HMM-based KO, PFAM and TIGRFAM annotations, as well as more sensitive diamond search mode. All had roughly equivalent accuracy (including on highly novel lineages) during cross-validation across multiple machine learning algorithms and configurations in predicting genome quality. However, HMM-based methods are substantially slower, which is a significant factor when having to annotate > 1 million synthetic genomes that form the basis of the CheckM2 models - now, and going forward into the future, as MAG databases expand rapidly. Given that it seemed that there was little accuracy to be gained from using HMM-search-based annotation methods, we went with DIAMOND-based KEGG annotation, as grouping KEGG's KO id's into pathways yielded a modest increase in accuracy and the entire approach was substantially faster. You're right that uniref100 is not comprehensive and KEGG-based IDs are a (much) smaller subsection of that, but even using alternative annotations such as STRING (diamond fast or ultra-sensitive search modes) yielded generally comparative accuracy despite a much bigger range of input vectors and/or higher alignment accuracy. We will continue testing accuracy in future CheckM2 releases and may switch to alternate annotation methods in future CheckM2 milestone releases (e.g. STRING or EggNOG) as databases expand and accuracy differences become more substantial, and especially as more high-quality genomes of unusual and novel lineages become available to more accurately gauge the effect of more distant protein homology. Hope this helps. Cheers, Alex

instruction: CheckM2 too conservative? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hi, I used checkM2 to check the completeness and contamination of some RefSeq complete genomes. I found that even if some genomes have an anomalous (extremely low) number of ribosomal proteins, checkM2 still mark them as high quality. Here are two examples. Accession | Name | Completeness | Contamination ---| ---| ---| --- GCF_016653575.1 |Bacillus sp. TK-2 | 98.77 | 0.08 GCF_014235785.1 | Bacillus sp. PAMC26568 | 92.50 | 0.00 CheckM1 gives 80.36% and 69.64% for these two genomes, which seems more reasonable. So I was wondering what could be the cause of this?
output: Hi, CheckM v1 and v2 are independent methods for assessing genome quality. CheckM v1 uses marker genes and thus is likely more sensitive to the presence or absence of ribosomal proteins. This is NOT necessarily good. GCF_016653575.1 has a genome size of 5.2 Mb which is nearly identical to the 98.9% complete genome GCF_006384875.1 which is the species cluster representative for these genomes in GTDB (see: https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org/genome?gid=GCF_006384875.1). This would suggest that GCF_016653575.1 is a fairly complete genome and perhaps has some assembly issues that are resulting in low number of ribosomal proteins being identified (?). GCF_014235785.1 has a genome size of 3.9 Mb. Other genomes in the same GTDB species cluster have a size of 4.6 Mb, 4.8 Mb, and 5.1 Mb and are all estimated to be >98% complete by CheckM v1. Genome size can vary substantially between strains of the same species, but on the surface it would seem that an estimate around 80% complete might be best so both the CheckM v1 and v2 estimates may be a bit inaccurate for this genome. Cheers, Donovan

Since you mentioned you're new on viral metagenomics, I would like to recommend you the following review on computational tools for the analysis of uncultivated phage genomes: https://doi.org/10.1128/mmbr.00004-21

input: Hi, Thanks for your nice tool. But I have a warning message of it, maybe because some contigs are large. instruction: Database path (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hi! I am working on a HPC environment. Checkm2 is added as an environmental module. Is there any other way of defining the path to the diamond database than using: $ checkm2 database --setdblocation This causes a permission error, since the command changes a config file physically (in a module, where I do not have writing permissions). As far as I can see, the program doesn't have a default database path. Best
output: Ehy @chklovski and @dparks1134 , This looks like it is the same issue from Checkm1 (see https://github.com/Ecogenomics/CheckM/pull/326) which has a simple fix: allow to save the configuration file somewhere else. I believe something like this should do the trick (with an extra CLI flag to get the config path): https://github.com/chklovski/CheckM2/blob/e563159f005344d798defb60cea4189e0be8ec92/checkm2/defaultValues.py#L5-L9 ```python class DefaultValues(): """Default values for filenames and common constants.""" def __init__(self, default_dir=None): if default_dir: self.default_dir = default_dir else: self.default_dir = os.path.dirname(__file__) DATA_PATH = os.path.join(self.default_dir , 'data') MODEL_PATH = os.path.join(self.default_dir , 'models') VERSION_PATH = os.path.join(self.default_dir , 'version') ```

instruction: Make Prodigal and Diamond outputs reusable. (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hello, Thank you for this great tool! I am currently adding the [metawrap bin_refinement](https://github.com/bxlab/metaWRAP#metagenomic-bin-recovery-improvements) module in a metagenomics analysis pipeline. This module uses CheckM1 and I would like to update it with CheckM2. The metawrap bin_refinement module works as follow: - it takes in input 3 bin sets made from different binning tools - it creates 4 new hybrid bin sets from the combination of each of them - then launch checkM on each bin set - finally it finds the best version of each bin in each bin set using checkM contamination and completeness. In this workflow, checkM is launched 7 times (on the 3 initial bin sets and on the 4 hybrid ones) but actually all the bin sets are from the same assembly which would make the Prodigal and Diamond step processing 7 times the same contigs. As far as I can see, it's the diamond step that take most of the time in checkM2 execution. So ideally I would like to be able to reuse Prodigal and Diamond outputs to analyze any other bin sets made from the same assembly. This would greatly reduce metawrap execution time! Thanks ! Jean
output: In my case contigs do not change (their sequences and ids do not change) but their repartitions in bins change making checkM2 prediction change as well. As the contigs do not change, it would be faster to launch Diamond only once and use its result to analyze all bin sets. I could try to make it work with the resume flag. For that I would need to launch prodigal and diamond on all contigs and shape their results specifically for each bin set mimicking what checkM2 would produced. Then I launch checkM2 with the resume flag and hopefully it will resume. It would not be the cleanest option but it would work.

instruction: scikit-learn upgrade? (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Hi, scikit-learn 0.23.2 is pretty outdated, could this (and maybe some of the other dependencies) be upgraded to a recent release? Thanks!
output: Hi, Scikit-learn in CheckM2 is used for a pre-trained MinMax scaler that transforms input data for the neural network input. The pickled scaler appears to break between versions, necessitating the requirement for a specific scikit-learn version. Future versions of CheckM2 may move away from using the scikit-learn scaler, but for now having a more outdated scikit-learn requirement doesn't affect anything within CheckM2. Tensorflow requirements may also be updated to a newer version in the future, but not before a major release that updates the prediction models. I would recommend having a separate conda environment for CheckM2 to avoid any conflicts for any other software.

instruction: CheckM2 missing files and result inconsistencies (Tool: CheckM2)
input: Great tool, but I have some questions. Is there a way to see, with checkM2, how many markers were used and found, and which ones these are? The output it gives seems very basic. Additionally, I am getting some inconsistent results and wanted to ask your opinion about them. Especially because it concerns DPANN and it was mentioned checkM2 works better for that group. From a Nanopore dataset of corrected reads with Canu, I made Flye assemblies. Once with supplying the corrected reads as --nano-raw and once as --nano-corr. These two assemblies (hereafter nano-raw and nano-corr) were used in metaWRAP for binning. The nano-raw assembly gives one Concoct bin (1 contig) of a DPANN, and a Maxbin2 bin (2 contigs) of the same DPANN. Basically, the maxbin2 bin is the contig present in the Concoct bin plus a 7.8kb contig. The nano-corr assembly gives one Concoct bin (1 contig) of a DPANN, and a Maxbin2 bin (2 contigs) of the same DPANN. Basically, the maxbin2 bin is the contig present in the Concoct bin plus a 7.8kb contig. The large contig of the bins the same size (2bp difference) in the different assemblies and they are 99.956% similar to each other. The 7.8kb contig present in the Maxbin2 bins of the different assemblies is identical. When I run checkM2, however, results seem a bit odd. nano-raw concoct bin: 60.96% complete; 0.54% contamination nano-raw Maxbin2 bin: 61.05% complete; 0.53% contamination nano-corr concoct bin: 60.74% complete; 0.63% contamination nano-corr Maxbin2 bin: 60.74% complete; 0.63% contamination Could you explain why that 7.8kb contig makes a difference in completeness in one of the bins, and not the other? The contig is identical, so potential changes should be the same. When I run checkM1 on the 4 bins, they all have a completeness of 64.65% and 0% contamination. Thanks a lot for the help/info on this!
output: Hi, CheckM2 does not use (single-copy) marker genes but a full set of KEGG protein annotation, as well as total counts of each amino acid, number of coding sequences, and length of genome as inputs. As a result, small variations in these inputs can produce minor variations in output as is seen here. As CheckM1 uses only single-copy marker genes, it is less likely to vary with small bin changes. Functionally your bin is 61% complete and 0.5% contaminated as estimated by CheckM2. Variations of <1% at this level of completeness is far below the error variance of CheckM2 (and even more of CheckM1) and is functionally meaningless (well below the resolution capacity of either tool). You can also check the 'diamond_output' folder which contains all KEGG annotations identified in any given bin/contig. If the KEGG annotations are the same, then the difference is down to minor changes in genomic properties (amino acid distribution, etc). Hope this answers your question.

instruction: Merged pair-members appear duplicated in the output (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, I just realized the 1.1.0 release and used it to join PE reads together using: `fastp --in1="$p"_R1.fastq.gz --in2="$p"_R2.fastq.gz --merge --merged_out="$p".fastp.fastq.gz --disable_adapter_trimming --dont_eval_duplication --length_required=200 --correction --overlap_len_require=30 --include_unmerged` Unfortunately, the merged reads appear twice. Am I using the cmdline args in a wrong way? I speculate it happens because of the `--include_unmerged` option, adding a sequence entry once again. instruction: "Expected '+', got" on FASTQ File Validated by seqkit sana (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, I'm encountering a reproducible crash with fastp when processing a some pairs of FASTQ files, similar to #534, #560, #565, #604 etc. I've used seqkit sana which reports the input file is valid, so I'm wondering if this might be a parsing edge case within fastp. Environment: fastp version: 0.24.1 Operating System: RHL 8.10 Command Used: fastp --in1 Preprocessing/mt.r1.fq --out1 Preprocessing/mt.r1.trimmed.fq --in2 Preprocessing/mt.r2.fq --out2 Preprocessing/mt.r2.trimmed.fq --unpaired1 Preprocessing/mt.se.trimmed.fq.tmp --unpaired2 Preprocessing/mt.se.trimmed.fq.tmp --detect_adapter_for_pe --qualified_quality_phred 15 --unqualified_percent_limit 40 --n_base_limit 5 --average_qual 0 --low_complexity_filter --complexity_threshold 30 --trim_front1 0 --trim_tail1 1 --cut_front --cut_front_window_size 1 --cut_front_mean_quality 30 --cut_tail --cut_tail_window_size 1 --cut_tail_mean_quality 30 --length_required 40 --overrepresentation_analysis --overrepresentation_sampling 20 --thread 64 --json Stats/mt/fastp.mt.json --html Stats/mt/fastp.mt.html>> logs/preprocessing/fastp_trimming.mt.log 2>&1 Observed Behavior: Detecting adapter sequence for read1... No adapter detected for read1 Detecting adapter sequence for read2... No adapter detected for read2 WARNING: fastp uses up to 16 threads although you specified 64 @A01275:33:HW5VTDSXY:3:1276:16125:35994 1:N:0:CTAGTGCTCT+TACTGTTCCA Expected '+', got ERROR: '+' expected Input File Validation: - Ensure no Windows CR or CRLF: grep -P '\r$' Preprocessing/mt.r*.fq - Validated with seqkit sana: seqkit sana Preprocessing/mt.r1.fq -o Preprocessing/mt.r1.rescued.fq Output: [INFO] File: Preprocessing/mt.r1.fq Pass records: 78467790 Discarded lines: 0 Let me know if youd want me to provide more than the attached fq sections mentioned in the error. [mt.r1.fq_prob_entry.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19869767/mt.r1.fq_prob_entry.txt) [mt.r2.fq_prob_entry.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19869766/mt.r2.fq_p
output: > I just did some more tests (with 0.24.1), and it seems like it was in fact a memory issue. Either allocating more >16 threads to fastp and/or lacking memory in general seems to have lead to the problem in my case. Sorry for the confusion. I could still share the files if youd like to check for yourself though. The thread number setting should not affect the FASTQ reading. It will still help if you can upload some complete FASTQ files that I can reproduce this issue. Thanks

instruction: Handling the non-linux line endings in fastq reader (Tool: FastP)
input: I am afraid that the FastqReader::getLine(string* line) function sometimes returns unexpected lines when encountering non-UNIX "\r\n" line endings. This may occur if any previous fastq manipulation was done in Windows. Consequently, the fastq reading fails entirely.
output: Dear Colleague, I am sending a link to two pairs of files: https://owncloud.cesnet.cz/index.php/s/4NrRd4NeJnKGqI4 Of these, 2068d fails with "Expected '+', got ERROR: '+' expected" whereas 2069a filters OK The command is fastp --in1 /media/sf_mikro/ngs_16s/43_lowca_zlobici_filtrace_fastp/sorted_staggering/01_r1_r2_trimmed_fastq_by_library/2069a_250301_S63_L001_R1_001.fastq --in2 /media/sf_mikro/ngs_16s/43_lowca_zlobici_filtrace_fastp/sorted_staggering/01_r1_r2_trimmed_fastq_by_library/2069a_250301_S63_L001_R2_001.fastq --trim_tail1 3 --trim_tail2 3 --disable_length_filtering --disable_adapter_trimming --qualified_quality_phred 15 --unqualified_percent_limit 1 --n_base_limit 0 --out1 /media/sf_mikro/ngs_16s/43_lowca_zlobici_filtrace_fastp/sorted_staggering/01B_r1_r2_filtered/2069a_250301_filtered_R1.fastq --out2 /media/sf_mikro/ngs_16s/43_lowca_zlobici_filtrace_fastp/sorted_staggering/01B_r1_r2_filtered/2069a_250301_filtered_R2.fastq --thread 20 --html /media/sf_mikro/ngs_16s/43_lowca_zlobici_filtrace_fastp/sorted_staggering/01B_r1_r2_filtered/2069a_250301_report.html After replacing the Windows line endings "\r\n" with the Linux style "\n" the problem was over. thank you very much! instruction: Memory leak with small fragment size libraries in v0.24.0 (Tool: FastP)
input: First off, I would like to thank you for developing and supporting FASTP for all these years. We've used it quite extensively, and its runtime performance and feature set is AMAZING. As a result of our FFPE DNA extraction and library prep protocol, we have been generating Illumina WGS data from libraries with very short fragment sizes (down to a mean of 100bp fragments in some really low quality samples). When we run FASTP on these samples we see memory usage absolutely explode (up to 100+GB, seems to increase as fragment size decreases) using the following command: `fastp --stdin --interleaved_in --length_required=30 --dont_eval_duplication --disable_quality_filtering --thread=5 --correction --out1 <fastq_R1> --out2 <fastq_R2> --adapter_fasta=<adapter.fa>--html=<html_path> --json=<json_path>` ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8af5e884-5b76-4f67-883e-f6b871d6cf10) We tried applying the memory leak patch in v0.24.0, and while memory usage did definitely improve, we are still seeing 100+GB for some samples. I am definitely not a C programmer but have been running Valgrind to try to find the source of this leak, and it has provided some insights: ``` ==2094941== Mismatched free() / delete / delete [] ==2094941== at 0x4C3A299: operator delete(void*, unsigned long) (vg_replace_malloc.c:935) ==2094941== by 0x16080B: PairEndProcessor::processPairEnd(ReadPack*, ReadPack*, ThreadConfig*) (in /home/user/anaconda3/envs/fastp/bin/fastp) ==2094941== by 0x162023: PairEndProcessor::processorTask(ThreadConfig*) (in /home/user/anaconda3/envs/fastp/bin/fastp) ==2094941== by 0x513AB6C: execute_native_thread_routine (thread.cc:104) ==2094941== by 0x4E4F1CE: start_thread (in /usr/lib64/libpthread-2.28.so) ==2094941== by 0x5604DD2: clone (in /usr/lib64/libc-2.28.so) ==2094941== Address 0x9a1b6b0 is 0 bytes inside a block of size 2,048 alloc'd ==2094941== at 0x4C38B6F: operator new[](unsigned long) (vg_replace_malloc.c:640) ==2094941== by 0x15D0EA: PairEndProcessor::read
output: I also ran `valgrind --leak-check=full --show-leak-kinds=all` on our CI sample, which provided quite verbose output: <details> <summary>Valgrind debugging on FASTP using CI sample</summary> ==2096077== Memcheck, a memory error detector ==2096077== Copyright (C) 2002-2024, and GNU GPL'd, by Julian Seward et al. ==2096077== Using Valgrind-3.24.0 and LibVEX; rerun with -h for copyright info ==2096077== Command: /shared/software/fastp-0.24.0-custom/fastp --stdin --interleaved_in --out1 /tmp/snakemake_nutcracker_ffpe_7743_R1.fastq --out2 /tmp/snakemake_nutcracker_ffpe_7743_R2.fastq --adapter_fasta=resources/truseq.fa.gz --correction --disable_quality_filtering --thread=2 --html=results/81-fastp/nutcracker_ffpe.fastp.html --json=results/81-fastp/nutcracker_ffpe.fastp.json --length_required=30 --dont_eval_duplication ==2096077== Read1 before filtering: total reads: 9685298 total bases: 1452794700 Q20 bases: 1200457104(82.6309%) Q30 bases: 1150074618(79.1629%) Read2 before filtering: total reads: 9685298 total bases: 1452794700 Q20 bases: 1200459447(82.631%) Q30 bases: 1150085739(79.1637%) Read1 after filtering: total reads: 9685298 total bases: 1449279806 Q20 bases: 1197926257(82.6567%) Q30 bases: 1147679598(79.1896%) Read2 after filtering: total reads: 9685298 total bases: 1449279806 Q20 bases: 1197891207(82.6542%) Q30 bases: 1147652176(79.1878%) Filtering result: reads passed filter: 19370596 reads failed due to low quality: 0 reads failed due to too many N: 0 reads failed due to too short: 0 reads with adapter trimmed: 92132 bases trimmed due to adapters: 7029788 reads corrected by overlap analysis: 316194 bases corrected by overlap analysis: 510499 Insert size peak (evaluated by paired-end reads): 269 ==2096077== Mismatched free() / delete / delete [] ==2096077== at 0x403E3B0: operator delete(void*) (vg_replace_malloc.c:1131) ==2096077== by 0x43D613: Stats::~Stats() (stats.cpp:111) ==2096077== by 0x4198AF: ThreadConfig::cleanup() (threadconfig.cpp:45) ==2096077== by 0x

instruction: (bug fixed)--overlapped_out option has a memory allocation issue (Tool: FastP)
input: First I want to say I enjoy reading your code. It's well-designed, well-organized and readable. Thank you for sharing this with the world! I've been working on customizing the code and came across a memory allocation problem. I think I tracked down the cause and a fix will be pull-requested soon. More specifically, after making the program on Github CodeSpace, I ran the following command for an output that contains an `--overlapped_out` option. I got the output of the stats and an error at the end, which will abort the program before the output on another computer. ``` $ ./fastp -i ./testdata/R1.fq -I ./testdata/R2.fq -m -o ./testdata/outm1.fq -O ./testdata/outm2.fq --merged_out ./testdata/outmerged2.fq --overlapped_out ./testdata/outstrictmerged2.fq Read1 before filtering: total reads: 9 total bases: 1208 Q20 bases: 1078(89.2384%) Q30 bases: 1005(83.1954%) Read2 before filtering: total reads: 9 total bases: 1359 Q20 bases: 1100(80.9419%) Q30 bases: 959(70.5666%) Merged and filtered: total reads: 8 total bases: 1552 Q20 bases: 1336(86.0825%) Q30 bases: 1256(80.9278%) Filtering result: reads passed filter: 16 reads failed due to low quality: 0 reads failed due to too many N: 0 reads failed due to too short: 2 reads with adapter trimmed: 0 bases trimmed due to adapters: 0 reads corrected by overlap analysis: 7 bases corrected by overlap analysis: 14 Duplication rate: 55.5556% Insert size peak (evaluated by paired-end reads): 187 Read pairs merged: 8 % of original read pairs: 88.8889% % in reads after filtering: 100% free(): invalid pointer Aborted (core dumped) ``` This seems to result from a duplicate deletion of pointers generated in `src/peprocessor.cpp` ``` line 462 Read* overlappedRead = new Read(r1->mName, new string(r1->mSeq->substr(max(0,ov.offset)), ov.overlap_len), r1->mStrand, new string(r1->mQuality->substr(max(0,ov.offset)), ov.overlap_len)); ``` Since `Read()` takes in the string name, `overlappedRead` takes the same `r1->mName` and `r1->mStrand` as the

instruction: interleaved output is not reproducible with multiple threads (Tool: FastP)
input: When writing to interleaved stdout with the --stdout flag, reads appear to be written in random order as md5sums from repeat runs differ. steps to reproduce with v0.23.3: ``` for TRY in 1 2 3; do fastp -w $THREADS --dont_eval_duplication -i in1.fq.gz -I in2.fq.gz -A -G -L -Q --stdout > interleave_attempt${TRY}.fastq; done md5sum interleave* for TRY in 1 2 3; do fastp -w $THREADS --dont_eval_duplication -i in1.fq.gz -I in2.fq.gz -A -G -L -Q -o split_attempt${TRY}_1.fastq -O split_attempt${TRY}_2.fastq; done md5sum split* ```
output: yes you fixed it! Excellent, thanks so much! Details of my test after building latest code from source: ``` RUN=r3 fastp --stdout --in1 grexome0050_1.fq.gz --in2 grexome0050_2.fq.gz --detect_adapter_for_pe --json /dev/null --html /dev/null --thread 16 2> fastp_${RUN}.log > grex50_${RUN}.fq RUN=r4 fastp --stdout --in1 grexome0050_1.fq.gz --in2 grexome0050_2.fq.gz --detect_adapter_for_pe --json /dev/null --html /dev/null --thread 16 2> fastp_${RUN}.log > grex50_${RUN}.fq # single thread: RUN=r5 fastp --stdout --in1 grexome0050_1.fq.gz --in2 grexome0050_2.fq.gz --detect_adapter_for_pe --json /dev/null --html /dev/null --thread 1 2> fastp_${RUN}.log > grex50_${RUN}.fq # compare: md5sum grex50_r*.fq 9cddf7dfb9377373817dc57adb45c8cb grex50_r3.fq 9cddf7dfb9377373817dc57adb45c8cb grex50_r4.fq 9cddf7dfb9377373817dc57adb45c8cb grex50_r5.fq ```

instruction: memory steadily increases as run continues (Tool: FastP)
input: There were other issues about memory, but none resolved/addressed what I am seeing. I run fastp 0.23.2 in a stream as follows. ``` fastp \ --stdin --interleaved_in --stdout \ --dont_eval_duplication \ --length_required 25 \ --merge --include_unmerged --correction \ --html mySample.html --json mySample.json \ --report_title mySample 2>/dev/null | ``` Memory usage continually climbs as long as the job runs. On large data sets I am getting OOM job kills on my cluster. The job below went from RES 4.9g to 5.3g as I typed this message... (%CPU is low at the moment I captured this because the downstream task is busy - fastp is, indeed, much faster than the aligner!). ``` PID USER PR NI VIRT RES SHR S %CPU %MEM TIME+ COMMAND 89783 wilsonte 20 0 5306760 4.9g 1828 S 2.0 2.6 49:47.81 fastp ``` Is this expected behavior? Memory leak? User error?
output: Until the fastp memory leak is fixed, some may find the following hack useful - would be better to fix the leak, of course. This perl script allows fastp to be used in a stream via intermittent calls that prevent memory accumulation. Save it to `buffered_fastp.pl` and use as shown. The presumption is that fastp will perform equally well on chunks of the total reads of a large data set. ```perl use strict; use warnings; # action: # apply fastp to single or interleaved reads in a FASTQ stream # buffer to multiple fastp calls to prevent memory overrun until fastp memory leak is fixed # requires environment variables: # FASTP_BUFFER # a system file on a fast directory to hold a buffered chunk of FASTQ reads # FASTP_BUFFER_N_READS # the number of reads to buffer into FASTP_BUFFER for each call to fastp # must be a multiple of 2 for interleaved paired reads # FASTP_COMMAND: # the fastp command you would have otherwise used in a data stream # this script will replace `--stdin` with `--in1 $FASTP_BUFFER` # be sure to set `--interleaved_in` if using paired reads # usage: # export FASTP_BUFFER=/path/to/buffer/file # export FASTP_BUFFER_N_READS=200000 # export FASTP_COMMAND="fastp --stdin [--interleaved_in] --stdout ..." # zcat *.fastq.gz | perl buffered_fastp.pl | ... $ENV{FASTP_COMMAND} =~ s/--stdin/--in1 $ENV{FASTP_BUFFER}/; open my $bufferH, ">", $ENV{FASTP_BUFFER} or die "could not open fastp write: $!\n"; my ($thisLineN0, $previousReadN1) = (0, 0); while (my $line = <STDIN>){ unless($thisLineN0 % 4){ if($previousReadN1 == $ENV{FASTP_BUFFER_N_READS}){ close $bufferH; print qx/$ENV{FASTP_COMMAND}/; open $bufferH, ">", $ENV{FASTP_BUFFER} or die "could not open fastp write: $!\n"; $previousReadN1 = 0; } $previousReadN1++; } print $bufferH $line; $thisLineN0++; } close $bufferH; print qx/$ENV{FASTP_COMMAND}/; unlink $ENV{FASTP_BUFFER}; ```

instruction: Segmentation Fault on interleaved fq files (Tool: FastP)

instruction: why the new version is slower (Tool: FastP)
input: fastp v0.20.0, time used: 358 seconds fastp v0.22.0, time used: 481 seconds The fastq file is the same Worker compute is the same Moreover, solid state drives and mechanical drives have no advantage in running speed.
output: > This may be caused by the new `duplication evaluation` feature, which is more accurate than previous versions. You can try to add `--dont_eval_duplication` option to disable duplication rate evaluation, and the speed will be back. > > In v0.23.0, the speed will be improved and be much faster than v0.20.0. v0.23.0 has not been released, but you can try it by downloading: http://opengene.org/fastp/fastp.0.23.0 fastp v0.23.0, time used: 496 seconds v0.23.0 memory use increase to 20GB， but run time not reduce.

instruction: How to pull by version? (Tool: FastP)
input: I built fastp in a docker image and it was working with v0.22.0 (it currently works on my local machine however I build that docker image over a week ago); however with issue #361 when I build a docker image in my CI/CD on AWS and then run a batch job I get the multithreading error. In my dockerfile I have, ``` RUN wget http://opengene.org/fastp/fastp && \ chmod a+x ./fastp && \ cp fastp /bin/fastp ``` Is it possible to pin v0.22.0? when I download from http://opengene.org/fastp/fastp?
output: I do the same now (I just recently changed it). I didn't know before checking this thread that specific versions were available just by specifying a tag after fastp. If they have always been available that way, then that's my bad. I just couldn't find an easy way to track releases and their changes online, which is helpful for troubleshooting and for dealing with version control in general. On Fri, Sep 24, 2021 at 3:29 PM Daniel Donovan ***@***.***> wrote: > I prefer it the way it is b/c I setup my dockerfile as: > > FROM ubuntu:20.04 > > ARG DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive > ARG VERSION=0.22.0 > > # Application installation > RUN wget -O fastp http://opengene.org/fastp/fastp.${VERSION} && \ > chmod a+x ./fastp && \ > cp fastp /bin/fastp > > — > You are receiving this because you commented. > Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub > <https://github.com/OpenGene/fastp/issues/362#issuecomment-926871155>, or > unsubscribe > <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AGBWOQROW6DYD6SWWVDJHCTUDTGTLANCNFSM5EQKDNCA> > . > Triage notifications on the go with GitHub Mobile for iOS > <https://apps.apple.com/app/apple-store/id1477376905?ct=notification-email&mt=8&pt=524675> > or Android > <https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.github.android&referrer=utm_campaign%3Dnotification-email%26utm_medium%3Demail%26utm_source%3Dgithub>. > > -- Jessica Rowell

instruction: Multithreading failed (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, With fastp 0.23.0, in a Docker image with Centos7 When using a simple command: ``` fastp --in1=Sample3.R1.fastq.gz --in2=Sample3.R2.fastq.gz --out1=Sample3.fastp.R1.fastq.gz --out2=Sample3.fastp.R2.fastq.gz ``` I obtain this error message: ``` terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::system_error' what(): Enable multithreading to use std::thread: Operation not permitted Aborted ``` I tried with "--thread=1", "--thread=2"... but same message. Apparently, it worked with fastp release 0.22.0. Do you have a solution?
output: So, I tried to compile. instruction: fastp does not read bash variable (Tool: FastP)
input: Hello, I'm attempting to run fastp like this: `READ1=test_R1` `READ2=test_R2` `fastp -i $READ1.fastq.gz -I $READ2.fastq.gz -o $READ1.qc.fastq.gz -O $READ2.qc.fastq.gz` However, fastp ignores the variables and attempts to find ".fastq.gz". I'm using the precompiled Linux binary. Guessing this is not the intended behavior so reporting here.
output: Hi @bowmanjeffs could u try use "" to surround the variables like following ```bash READ1=test_R1 READ2=test_R2 fastp -i "$READ1.fastq.gz" -I "$READ2.fastq.gz" -o "$READ1.qc.fastq.gz" -O "$READ2.qc.fastq.gz" ```

instruction: Idea: add function for QC only (Tool: FastP)
input: Could you consider the adding disable option at once? `--disable_trim_poly_g --disable_adapter_trimming --disable_quality_filtering –disable_length_filtering ` No need trim/filtering function for QC.
output: If QC only, you can just do not set the output files.

instruction: FASTP output fails to align using STAR (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, I have tried to trim and filter reads by length using Fastp, but to no avail. The program runs successfully, but my downstream alignment results is near-zero aligment percentage (using STAR, a popular RNA-seq aligner) . I cannot see any problem in Fastp's output FASTQ format, and using other programs the same samples are successfuly aligned after trimming and length filtering. Any input on the matter would be appreciated, once this is resolved I would like to incorporate Fastp in all my analysis pipelines. Example of reads which could not be aligned after processing: **Mate 1** `@SRR3184286.sra.1259451 1259451 length=100 GCCTGAGCACACACACACATTCCACACGCATCATTAAAGGATAGCCATTTATTCTTCATAGATCGGAAGAGCACACGTCTGAACTCCAGTCACGT` `+SRR3184286.sra.1259451 1259451 length=100 BBBFFFFFFFFFFIIIIIIF0BFIIIFIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIFIIIFIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFBBBFFBFFFFF` `@SRR3184286.sra.1259452 1259452 length=100 TCTCAACACCCGCCCCCCCCATGTTTAAACTTTGTGCCTTTGACCATCTCTTAGGTCTAATGATATTTTATGCAAACAGTTCTTGGCCCCCTGAA` `+SRR3184286.sra.1259452 1259452 length=100 <BBBBBBBBBBFFBFFIFFFF<<FFF<BF<BFFB<<<B<BB<BB7B<<0<BB''<07<<0B070<BBBBFBBBF<<0700<<0<0''00077000` **Mate 2** `@SRR3184286.sra.1259451 1259451 length=100 GCCTGAGCACACACACACATTCCACACGCATCATTAAAGGATAGCCATTTATTCTTCATAGATCGGAAGAGCACACGTCTGAACTCCAGTCACGT +SRR3184286.sra.1259451 1259451 length=100 BBBFFFFFFFFFFIIIIIIF0BFIIIFIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIFIIIFIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFBBBFFBFFFFF @SRR3184286.sra.1259452 1259452 length=100 TCTCAACACCCGCCCCCCCCATGTTTAAACTTTGTGCCTTTGACCATCTCTTAGGTCTAATGATATTTTATGCAAACAGTTCTTGGCCCCCTGAA +SRR3184286.sra.1259452 1259452 length=100 <BBBBBBBBBBFFBFFIFFFF<<FFF<BF<BFFB<<<B<BB<BB7B<<0<BB''<07<<0B070<BBBBFBBBF<<0700<<0<0''00077000` And after processing: **Mate 1** `@SRR3184286.sra.1259451 0:N: 00 GCCTGAGCACACACACACATTCCACACGCATCATTAAAGGATAGCCATTTATTCTTCATAGATCGGAAGAGCACACGTCTGAACTCCAGTCACGT` `+` `BBBFFFFFFFFFFIIIIIIF0BFIIIFIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIFIIIFIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFBBBFFBFFFFF` `@SRR3184286.
output: The program does not fail, but simply aligns under 1% of the reads. Using a different trimming program, the preprocessed reads had over 80% alignment. Only ~400 reads are aligned, from a 1317000 read pool. I tried to look at the FASTQ format, used two different versions of Fastp and removed all possible dependancies, but nothing seemed to help. Perhaps you have an idea why. [SRR3184285Log.out.txt](https://github.com/OpenGene/fastp/files/5736335/SRR3184285Log.out.txt) [SRR3184285Log.final.out.txt](https://github.com/OpenGene/fastp/files/5736336/SRR3184285Log.final.out.txt)

instruction: fastp for mac os (Tool: FastP)
input: Hey, In my R package ORFik I use fastp combined with STAR to align data, as this is the fastest combo I can find. But sadly mac users need to do their own trimming since fastp is not supported on mac os. Any plans to make it run on Mac ? :)
output: For now, I got it to work on mac (I just automatized for the user: wget fastp + unzip + make), until I figure a way to get STAR rna aligner to work on windows, there is no point for me to get fastp to work there. I will think about the submodule. Thanks for the info, I will close this issue, I have the answers for my questions.

input: May it be possible to add an option to run only the quality checking and generate the report?

instruction: [Feature request] Process a part of reads only (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi， In some case, we want to process only a part of reads instead of the whole reads in fastq file, such as the top 1000000 reads of each fastq file, this cant help us to quickly get an overview of the reads quality , and this can also be used as an datasize controller of reads which used in downstream analysis. output: Hi, it's already supported in fastp. Please use `--reads_to_process` option

instruction: Fastq files with MS-DOS end-line break (Tool: FastP)
input: Hello, So I have been running fastp and it has worked previously, but recently I ran a sample where roughly 100K reads out of 1M reads were trimmed and then fastp stops with the message: ``` ERROR: sequence and quality have different length: @read name Read Sequence + ``` I did dig a bit in the fastqreader code and it seems that there's some offset by 1 here so that the separator line now is empty and the separator instead occupies the line for quality. I did find that the file has ^M$ at the end of each line indicating the file has been saved on a windows machine. By looking at the getline function in the fastqreader it seems you tried to deal with this, but it seems the problem has persisted.

instruction: umi trimming and deduplication (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi I used fastp to trim out umi seqeunce by using the option below, `fastp -i R1_001.fastq.gz -I R2_001.fastq.gz -o R1_001_umi.fastq.gz -OR2_001_umi.fastq.gz -U --umi_loc=per_read --umi_len=4 --umi_skip=1 -g –Q --adapter_sequence=AGATCGGAAGAGCACACGTCTGAACTCCAGTCA --adapter_sequence_r2=AGATCGGAAGAGCGTCGTGTAGGGAAAGAGTGT` De-duplication step was already done by this command? or do I need other step for de-duplication? Thanks! HJ
output: > De-duplication from FASTQ level is not supported by fastp yet. > > You can use the BAM file to deduplicate using our other tool gencore https://github.com/OpenGene/gencore Fastp is quite fast, and in some cases，such as metagenomics which is hard to use reference, I really hope your team can consider adding this feature. As far as I konw, the other de novo de-duplication software is quite slow to run in metagenomics. Otherwise, I am wondering since fastp can identify the duplication, why would not mark it in the sequence header or remove it? I guess maybe the software identify the duplication without considering all reads? Thanks a lot.

instruction: Why is 270 the maximum measurement limit for "Insert size estimation" ?? (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, It was very convenient when I used fastp. but, my report says "88.524713% reads are with unknown length". Certainly, since I experimented with a fragmentation size of 350 and 150PE, most reads would be longer than 270bp. Why is 270 the maximum measurement limit for "Insert size estimation" ?? ![newplot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43412957/71089706-c7688f80-21e4-11ea-9107-132f6c653c85.png)
output: Because fastp need at least 30 bp overlap to calculate the insert size of a DNA fragment. 300 - 30 = 270

instruction: fastp "Error to read gzip file" (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, I'm trying to run fastp on WES .fastq.gz files but for each of them i get "Error to read gzip file". The code im running is > cd $WES/input/ > for f in *_R1*; do > export R1="$(basename "$f")" > export R2=`echo "$(basename "$f")" | sed 's/_R1/_R2/'` > echo "found" "$R1" "$R2" > done > echo "input files are" "$R1" "$R2" > > cd $WES/output > fastp -i $R1 -o ${R1%%.*}_trimmed.fastq.gz -I $R2 -O ${R2%%.*}_trimmed.fastq.gz --unpaired1 ${R1%%.*}_unpair.fastq --unpaired2 ${R2%%.*}_unpair.fastq --failed_out ${R1%%_*}_failed.fastq -c --detect_adapter_for_pe -h fasp.html --dont_overwrite -z 9 -w 8 I'd really like to use gziped files rather than decomp and raw fastq. Is that an error on my side or else ? fastqc and trimmomatic used these files perfectly fine, but not fastp. P.S. github detects some part of code as BBcodes so dont mind it please
output: Okay, seems like fastp dont like first part of code where it scans for R1/R2 files to use them later on. When i manually written path and name it worked. Guess i have to figure out how to rewrite it.

instruction: Up to 16 threads? (Tool: FastP)
input: Actually it uses only 4-6 cores in real datasets no matter how many threads I assigned. Why limiting it to 16 cores only? How can I use 128 threads on my linux server.
output: same issue. I am using a cluster with client servers that each have 48 cores with hyperthreading up to 192 and want to trim PE fastq files that have ~ 400 million reads. I am reading from a dedicated raid 0 striped server which mitigates the I/O issue, but this tool ends up taking way longer than Trimmomatic, simply due to the time that it is taking to read the files and limiting its use to 16 threads.

instruction: Feature request: allow multiple files to be input at once (Tool: FastP)
input: Often sequencing files come in parts, it would be convenient to able to specify a list of files to be read by `-i` or `-I`. Thanks for all your work!

instruction: Makefile enhancements (Tool: FastP)
input: The patch below adds DESTDIR support and an install-strip target. This will facilitate installing fastp via package managers, most of which can use these features by default. Also suggesting a slightly cleaner way to amend CXXFLAGS, but it works for me either way. It was pretty easy already, though - thanks for making fastp simple and portable to begin with! ``` --- Makefile.orig 2019-04-17 03:23:22 UTC +++ Makefile @@ -3,9 +3,11 @@ DIR_SRC := ./src DIR_OBJ := ./obj PREFIX ?= /usr/local +DESTDIR ?= BINDIR ?= $(PREFIX)/bin INCLUDE_DIRS ?= LIBRARY_DIRS ?= +STRIP_CMD ?= strip SRC := $(wildcard ${DIR_SRC}/*.cpp) OBJ := $(patsubst %.cpp,${DIR_OBJ}/%.o,$(notdir ${SRC})) @@ -15,7 +17,8 @@ TARGET := fastp BIN_TARGET := ${TARGET} CXX ?= g++ -CXXFLAGS := -std=c++11 -g -O3 -I${DIR_INC} $(foreach includedir,$(INCLUDE_DIRS),-I$(includedir)) ${CXXFLAGS} +CXXFLAGS ?= -g -O3 +CXXFLAGS += -std=c++11 -I${DIR_INC} $(foreach includedir,$(INCLUDE_DIRS),-I$(includedir)) LIBS := -lz -lpthread LD_FLAGS := $(foreach librarydir,$(LIBRARY_DIRS),-L$(librarydir)) $(LIBS) $(LD_FLAGS) @@ -38,5 +41,8 @@ make_obj_dir: fi install: - install $(TARGET) $(BINDIR)/$(TARGET) + install $(TARGET) $(DESTDIR)$(BINDIR)/$(TARGET) @echo "Installed." + +install-strip: install + $(STRIP_CMD) $(DESTDIR)$(BINDIR)/$(TARGET) ```
output: Patches improved and updated for 0.23.1: ``` --- Makefile.orig 2021-10-19 02:19:29 UTC +++ Makefile @@ -2,11 +2,13 @@ DIR_INC := ./inc DIR_SRC := ./src DIR_OBJ := ./obj -PREFIX ?= /usr/local -BINDIR ?= $(PREFIX)/bin -INCLUDE_DIRS ?= -LIBRARY_DIRS ?= +PREFIX ?= /usr/local +BINDIR ?= $(PREFIX)/bin +INCLUDE_DIRS ?= +LIBRARY_DIRS ?= +STRIP ?= strip + SRC := $(wildcard ${DIR_SRC}/*.cpp) OBJ := $(patsubst %.cpp,${DIR_OBJ}/%.o,$(notdir ${SRC})) @@ -15,15 +17,19 @@ TARGET := fastp BIN_TARGET := ${TARGET} CXX ?= g++ -CXXFLAGS := -std=c++11 -pthread -g -O3 -I${DIR_INC} $(foreach includedir,$(INCLUDE_DIRS),-I$(includedir)) ${CXXFLAGS} +# Optional flags that the user can override by setting CXXFLAGS in the +# env or make argument +CXXFLAGS ?= -g -O3 +# Required flags +CXXFLAGS += -std=c++11 -I${DIR_INC} $(foreach includedir,$(INCLUDE_DIRS),-I$(includedir)) LIBS := -lisal -ldeflate -lpthread STATIC_FLAGS := -static -Wl,--no-as-needed -pthread -LD_FLAGS := $(foreach librarydir,$(LIBRARY_DIRS),-L$(librarydir)) $(LIBS) $(LD_FLAGS) +# Append required flags to standard LDFLAGS from env +LDFLAGS += $(foreach librarydir,$(LIBRARY_DIRS),-L$(librarydir)) $(LIBS) STATIC_LD_FLAGS := $(foreach librarydir,$(LIBRARY_DIRS),-L$(librarydir)) $(STATIC_FLAGS) $(LIBS) $(STATIC_LD_FLAGS) - ${BIN_TARGET}:${OBJ} - $(CXX) $(OBJ) -o $@ $(LD_FLAGS) + $(CXX) $(OBJ) -o $@ $(LDFLAGS) static:${OBJ} $(CXX) $(OBJ) -o ${BIN_TARGET} $(STATIC_LD_FLAGS) @@ -49,6 +55,11 @@ make_obj_dir: mkdir $(DIR_OBJ) ; \ fi +# Respect DESTDIR for staged installs (used by most package managers) install: - install $(TARGET) $(BINDIR)/$(TARGET) + install $(TARGET) $(DESTDIR)$(BINDIR)/$(TARGET) @echo "Installed." + +# Many package managers use install-strip target if debugging is not enabled +install-strip: install + $(STRIP) $(DESTDIR)$(BINDIR)/$(TARGET) --- src/main.cpp.orig 2021-10-19 02:19:29 UTC +++ src/main.cpp @@ -1,4 +1,5 @@ #include <stdio.h> +#include <sysexits.h> #include "fastqreader.h" #include "unittest.h" #include <ti

instruction: Default options not working (Tool: FastP)
input: I'm trimming some data from ENA with fastp. Here is the dataset https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/data/view/SRR5525985 and the command line I'm using `fastp -i SRR5525985_1.fastq.gz -I SRR5525985_2.fastq.gz -o SRR5525985_1.tr.fastq.gz -O SRR5525985_2.tr.fastq.gz` fastp version 0.20.0 However, the output is not as expected. The trimmed reads still have a similar dropoff in quality as the untrimmed reads. The fastp.html file produced is [here](https://www.dropbox.com/s/qm4a72urrees3ov/fastp.html?dl=0) (dropbox link as github doesn't allow html upload). I'm probably just reading the options incorrectly, but I expected the output to be high quality (> Q20) to the end of the read. Can you help?
output: Yes # per read cutting by quality score `fastp` supports per read sliding window cutting by evaluating the mean quality scores in the sliding window. From `v0.19.6`, `fastp` supports 3 different operations, and you enable one or all of them: * `-5, --cut_front` move a sliding window from front (5') to tail, drop the bases in the window if its mean quality is below cut_mean_quality, stop otherwise. Default is disabled. The leading N bases are also trimmed. Use `cut_front_window_size` to set the widnow size, and `cut_front_mean_quality` to set the mean quality threshold. If the window size is 1, this is similar as the Trimmomatic `LEADING` method. * `-3, --cut_tail` move a sliding window from tail (3') to front, drop the bases in the window if its mean quality is below cut_mean_quality, stop otherwise. Default is disabled. The trailing N bases are also trimmed. Use `cut_tail_window_size` to set the widnow size, and `cut_tail_mean_quality` to set the mean quality threshold. If the window size is 1, this is similar as the Trimmomatic `TRAILING` method. * `-r, --cut_right` move a sliding window from front to tail, if meet one window with mean quality < threshold, drop the bases in the window and the right part, and then stop. Use `cut_right_window_size` to set the widnow size, and `cut_right_mean_quality` to set the mean quality threshold. This is similar as the Trimmomatic `SLIDINGWINDOW` method. ***WARNING: all these three operations will interfere deduplication for SE data, and `--cut_front` or `--cut_right` may also interfere deduplication for PE data. The deduplication algorithms rely on the exact matchment of coordination regions of the grouped reads/pairs.*** If `--cut_right` is enabled, then there is no need to enable `--cut_tail`, since the former is more aggressive. If `--cut_right` is enabled together with `--cut_front`, `--cut_front` will be performed first before `--cut_right` to avoid dropping whole reads due to the low quality starting bases. Please be not

instruction: Distinct output file for merged PE reads (Tool: FastP)
input: Hi, thanks for recently adding the merging feature of PE reads #139 ! Would it be possible to implement a 3rd output parameter in order to separate the merged/unmerged reads in an intuitive way like assemblers (e.g. SPAdes) ask for them? So, we could immediately end up with cleaned R1, R2 and merged read files. For example: ``` bash fastp -i R1.fastq.gz -I R2.fastq.gz -o R1.clean.fastq.gz -O R2.clean.fastq.gz --out_merged R12.clean.fastq.gz ``` Maybe worth of a distinct issue but somehow related to this: What happens with either R1 or R2 reads resulting from successful filtering procedures but lacking a related mate? Could these also be written to a distinct file, like SE reads? Trimmomatic is able to behave that way and thus, those valid reads can be fed into downstream analyses.
output: Hi, a follow up...hopefully this clarifies what I am trying to ask/suggest. Below you find an extract from the SPAdes manual of how to provide PE data: ``` Paired-end libraries --pe<#>-1 <file_name> File with left reads for paired-end library number <#> (<#> = 1,2,..,9). --pe<#>-2 <file_name> File with right reads for paired-end library number <#> (<#> = 1,2,..,9). --pe<#>-m <file_name> File with merged reads from paired-end library number <#> (<#> = 1,2,..,9) If the properties of the library permit, paired reads can be merged using special software. Non-empty files with (remaining) unmerged left/right reads (separate or interlaced) must be provided for the same library for SPAdes to correctly detect the original read length. --pe<#>-s <file_name> File with unpaired reads from paired-end library number <#> (<#> = 1,2,..,9) For example, paired reads can become unpaired during the error correction procedure. ``` Currently, fastp can serve the R1 (pe-1) and R2 (pe-2) reads after filtering and recently, also merged R12 (pe-m). In order to utilize as much genome information as possible, it would be great if fastp could: 1. apply the various quality filters and correction 2. try to merge R1 and R2 3. write merged R12 (pe-m) reads to one file, e.g. m.fastq 4. write unmerged paired reads to R1 (pe-1) and R2 (pe-2) files, e.g. R1.fastq, R2.fastq 5. write unmerged unpaired reads (pe-s) to another file, e.g. s.fastq If I got it right, in step 5 R2 reads would have to be reverse complimented before writing them to s.fastq along with residual R1 reads. Unforutnately, I dont have much knowledge about fastp internals, but by this procedure, fastp could provide merged, paired and unpaired reads in a single step. This would definetly be very beneficial for assembies. What do you think? Thanks a lot for considering and best regards!

instruction: Option to merge overlapping PE reads? (Tool: FastP)
input: From the main README file, you already have code to look for PE overlaps and use this for correcting mismatches & detecting adapters. Could you add an option to output only the merged reads? Right now I would only want the overlaps as output (not interested in the non-overlapping reads). This could replace the need to call a separate FASTQ pair-merging tool like ``pear`` or ``flash``, https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3933873/ https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3198573/
output: Hi guys, this function is implemented, please have a try and help to update this thread with the results. # merge paired-end reads For paired-end (PE) input, fastp supports stiching them by specifying the `-m/--merge` option. In this `merging` mode, the output will be a single file. In the output file, a tag will `merged_xxx_yyy` be added to the read name to indicate that how many base pairs are from read1 and from read2, respectively. For example, ` @NB551106:9:H5Y5GBGX2:1:22306:18653:13119 1:N:0:GATCAG merged_150_15` means that 150bp are from read1, and 15bp are from read2. `fastp` prefers the bases in read1 since they usually have higher quality than read2. For the pairs of reads that cannot be merged successfully, they will be both included in the output by default. But you can specify the `--chastity` option to discard the unmerged reads. Same as the base correction feature, this function is also based on overlapping detection, which has adjustable parameters `overlap_len_require (default 30)` and `overlap_diff_limit (default 5)`.

instruction: Blank/missing reads in FASTQ cause malformed output FASTQ (Tool: FastP)
input: If one of the input FASTQ files has a missing/blank sequence, the output FASTQ will be malformed. I.e. if the input FASTQ has a correct entry followed by a blank entry like this: ``` @A00545 ACGTGCTGAC + FFF,:,FFFF @A00546 + ``` where the sequence line and quality line are both blank, then the output will have a malformed entry, like so, where there is only a "+" and a quality sequence, but no read ID or genetic sequence: ``` @A00545 ACGTGCTGAC + FFF,:,FFFF + F:FFFFFFFF ``` The program should either throw an error if the FASTQ file has a blank entry, or skip the entry entirely in the output. (In this case, I didn't know my FASTQ file had a blank entry—it came off the sequencer like that—but Fastp ran with no errors and I only discovered the problem during downstream analysis because the output FASTQ file was not correctly formed.)
output: No, this will not fix the issue. You can see in my example, on line 5, there is a correct "@" symbol and valid ID line, but the sequence line and the quality sequence line are empty, and in the output, it gives only 2 lines for that entry instead of 4 lines, so the whole output is wrong. So it needs to check that those are not empty, you cannot just check the header name.

instruction: shouldn't poly A trimming happen after adaptor trimming? (Tool: FastP)
